project_overview.project_name: Payments Automation and Integration of Salaries in Afghanistan (PAISA)
project_overview.project_development_objective: To support the development of digital government-to-person payments in Afghanistan
project_overview.total_financing: US$45 million (IDA: US$10 million / ARTF: US$35 million)
project_overview.beneficiaries: Afghan civil servants (400,439 employees), pensioners (152,909), Martyrs and Disabled beneficiaries (281,114), and broader Afghan population through expanded digital financial services access

primary_locations: Nationwide coverage planned across all provinces
Initial focus: Kabul, Kandahar, Nangarhar, Parwan provinces
Phased rollout by provinces based on readiness and security conditions
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: yes - Afghanistan is a fragile and conflict-affected state with significant security challenges. Civilian casualties at unprecedented levels (10,459 killed/wounded in 2017, 10,993 in 2018). Some areas remain difficult to access due to insecurity. Project design includes resilience measures for conflict-prone areas.

key_components.component_name: Component 1: Biometric identification system for civil servants and pensioners
key_components.description: Support for biometric registration and issuance of unique registration numbers (URN) to civil servants and pensioners, managed by NSIA. Includes legal/regulatory framework development, infrastructure upgrades, data migration from existing registries, enrollment operations, data security measures, and disaster recovery site establishment.

key_components.component_name: Component 2: Integration of the Identification, Verification and Payment Systems
key_components.description: Integration of biometric ID system with AFMIS (Afghanistan Financial Management Information System), HRMIS (Human Resource Management Information System), ATS (Automated Transfer System), and APS (Afghanistan Payment System) to achieve straight-through processing of salary payments. Includes system architecture design, payment workflow enhancement, and institutional strengthening at MoF and DAB.

key_components.component_name: Component 3: Expansion of financial services and access points
key_components.description: Support for expansion of cash-out points (ATMs, agents, branches) and cashless payment acceptance through performance-based incentives to financial institutions. Includes development of access point expansion strategy, agent monitoring platform, and communications/outreach activities to promote digital payments.
Project coordination, administration, social and fiduciary aspects. Includes staffing, monitoring and evaluation, baseline surveys, field monitoring, and grievance redress mechanism implementation.

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Ministry of Finance (MoF) and Da Afghanistan Bank (DAB). MoF responsible for Components 1, 2a, and 4. DAB responsible for Components 2b and 3. No separate PIU established; integration into existing structures.
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Three-tier structure: (1) Joint Coordination Committee (Steering Committee) chaired by MoF at Deputy Minister level; (2) Core Reform Implementation Team (CRIT) comprised of MoF, DAB, NSIA, CSC, and Asan Khedmat, chaired by MoF; (3) PAISA Project Implementation Team with focal points from MoF and DAB. Presidential Decrees (No. 264 dated April 22, 2018 and March 11, 2019) formalize coordination framework.
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank Procurement Regulations for IPF Borrowers (July 2016, revised November 2017 and August 2018). Competitive selection of financial institutions for non-consulting services. Project Procurement Strategy for Development (PPSD) prepared. Use of STEP (Systematic Tracking of Exchanges in Procurement) system. Prior review by World Bank. Post-procurement review annually. Emphasis on transparency, publication on NPA website, UNDB, and national newspapers.
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: Strong M&E framework with quarterly and semi-annual reporting. Baseline survey at project effectiveness, midterm review (24 months), and end-project evaluation. Beneficiary feedback mechanism integrated. Third-party monitoring (TPM) for ARTF-funded activities producing quarterly reports. Gender-disaggregated data collection. Citizen Engagement (CE) indicators included in results framework. Grievance Redress Mechanism (GRM) established by MoF and MCIT/Asan Khedmat.

identified_risks.risk_description: Possibility of reversal of policy commitment and failure to implement necessary reforms (national identification). 2019 Presidential elections and political instability could negatively impact growth, investments, and fiscal outlook. Multiplicity of actors in mobile salary payments increases coordination risks.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: GoIRA's commitment to coordinate reforms through Presidential Decrees establishing multi-agency coordination committee. Broad-based multi-stakeholder consensus on approach. Close consultation with Core Reform Implementation Team. Continued coordination during implementation to solidify advocacy and support.

identified_risks.risk_description: Deteriorating security environment with civilian casualties at unprecedented levels. Some areas remain difficult to access due to insecurity. Armed conflict and terrorism pose risks to biometric registration efforts, agent network development, and digital payment processing. Vulnerability of mobile phone networks and electricity provision to terrorism and conflict.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Phased geographical implementation accounting for security conditions and operational readiness. Design of resilient business continuity plans with redundancy. Disaster recovery site establishment. Backup procedures for offline payment processing. Flexible enrollment approaches (mobile units, multiple registration points).

identified_risks.risk_description: Heterogeneous capacity of entities involved in salary payments. Large number of civil servants across diverse agencies with varying levels of collaboration willingness. Lack of sufficiently qualified personnel at MoF for core technical activities. Scarcity of IT talent with cybersecurity expertise.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Sequencing and phasing of implementation based on readiness. Capacity building of institutions through technical expertise hiring and international expert deployment. Recruitment of additional staff at MoF and DAB. Utilization of off-the-shelf IT products and outsourced service delivery. Technical assistance and institutional strengthening activities. Knowledge exchange with relevant countries.

identified_risks.risk_description: Lack of sufficiently qualified personnel at MoF to implement core technical project activities. Weak overall control environment noted in PEFA assessment. Insufficient control systems for payroll processing.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Hiring and training of required staff for fiduciary functions. Centralization of procurement at MoF. Reliance on country systems with comprehensive Financial Management Manual. Internal audit by MoF and DAB departments. External audit by Supreme Audit Office. Regular FM reviews by World Bank (twice annually). Maintenance of adequate books of records and supporting documentation.

identified_risks.risk_description: Complexity of integrating multiple systems (HRMIS, AFMIS, ATS, APS, biometric ID system). Reliance on reforms undertaken in other related areas (TAGHIR for HRMIS development). Risk of delays in HRMIS operationalization. Limited mobile phone network coverage and electricity provision challenges. Regulatory environment gaps, particularly regarding data protection.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Close coordination with TAGHIR and other World Bank engagements. Development of comprehensive legal and regulatory framework for biometric ID system. System architecture design and implementation roadmap development. Business continuity plans with redundancy. Extensive consultations with industry to address concerns. Development of data protection and privacy frameworks aligned with international standards.

identified_risks.risk_description: Possible lack of adoption and uptake by civil servants due to literacy, trust issues, or resistance to re-enrollment given pre-existing biometric registration initiatives. Association with e-Tazkera may create negative perceptions. Lack of uptake from population after incentives are withdrawn.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Roll-out of comprehensive communications and outreach campaign in collaboration with stakeholders. Awareness-raising activities about benefits and consumer protection measures. Targeted outreach to women. Development of attractive value proposition for digital payments. Phased withdrawal of incentives based on sliding scale principle. Surveys and beneficiary feedback mechanisms.

identified_risks.risk_description: High dependence on foreign aid (46% of GDP in 2017, expected to decline to 20% by 2030). Weak economic growth (2% projected for 2018). Poverty rate increased from 38% (2011/12) to 55% (2016/17). Business confidence declining. Fiscal uncertainties.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project embedded in comprehensive transformational policy agenda supported by policy-based financing (IP DPG) and IMF Extended Credit Facility. High-level government commitment to reforms. Coordination with broader civil service and payments reform agenda.

identified_risks.risk_description: Risk that implementation progress would be affected by deficiencies in regulatory environment. Gaps in data protection and privacy legal framework. Possibility that some areas remain uncovered by adequate network of payment service providers despite incentive programs.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Development of comprehensive legal and regulatory framework for biometric ID system. Reliance on international standards and best practices. Extensive market consultations. Development of access point expansion strategy based on market research. Performance-based incentives with transparent, publicly-available eligibility criteria.

identified_risks.risk_description: Scarcity of IT talent with deep knowledge of cybersecurity. Limited availability of qualified personnel for implementation of IT-intensive activities.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Utilization of off-the-shelf IT products. Exploration of outsourced service delivery. Investments to build internal capacity of government agencies. Incentivization of hiring of skilled graduates. Technical assistance and institutional strengthening including short-term expertise hiring. Dedicated TA to NSIA and DAB on data security and privacy.

safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: yes - Project-wide effective GRM established by MoF in coordination with MCIT/Asan Khedmat to address grievances related to project activities (biometric registration, salary payment process). GRM to serve beyond project life for overall system grievances. Asan Khedmat mandated to develop and deploy comprehensive GRM mechanism. Quarterly reporting on grievances addressed. Target: 80% of grievances addressed within stipulated time. World Bank's corporate Grievance Redress Service (GRS) also available for project-affected communities.
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Significant gender gap in financial inclusion addressed through project design. Only 7.2% of Afghan women own transaction accounts vs. 15% overall. Only 2.6% of women received digital payments in past year vs. 9% overall. Project supports: (1) Gender-sensitive biometric registration with female enrollment agents where feasible; (2) Development of female agent networks in conservative rural areas to exclusively serve female clients; (3) Targeted outreach to women on digital payments benefits; (4) Ensuring payment points serve women throughout country; (5) Gender-disaggregated data collection in M&E; (6) Selection criteria for access point expansion to ensure appropriate share of women-owned businesses. Female civil servants and pensioners tracked separately in results framework.
vulnerable_groups: Civil servants paid through cash (40% of payroll) - often in remote/insecure areas
Women civil servants and pensioners (disproportionately affected by financial exclusion)
Returnees from Pakistan and Iran (2 million since 2015)
Populations in conflict-affected and insecure areas
Populations in remote/underserved areas with limited financial access

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project explicitly designed with conflict sensitivity. Addresses security risks through: (1) Digital payments reducing need for cash handling on paydays, reducing security risks and costs; (2) Phased geographical implementation accounting for security conditions; (3) Resilient system design with business continuity plans and disaster recovery; (4) Offline payment processing capabilities for connectivity outages; (5) Multiple payment channels reducing single-point-of-failure risks; (6) Interoperable system design preventing dependency on single provider. Project recognizes that armed conflict and deteriorated security have rendered cash transactions insecure and costly. Digital payments provide safer alternative for government and beneficiaries.
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Afghanistan is a fragile and conflict-affected state with multiple fragility factors: (1) Ongoing armed conflict with Taliban and other groups; (2) Civilian casualties at unprecedented levels (10,459 in 2017, 10,993 in 2018); (3) Weak state institutions and limited government capacity; (4) High dependence on foreign aid (46% of GDP); (5) Poverty rate increased to 55% (2016/17); (6) Low financial inclusion (9 out of 10 adults financially excluded); (7) Weak rule of law and high corruption (ranked 177/180 on Corruption Perception Index); (8) Limited private sector base; (9) Vulnerability to natural disasters and climate change; (10) Political instability around 2019 elections; (11) Declining business confidence; (12) Some areas difficult to access due to insecurity. Project contributes to state-building through strengthening core government institutions (MoF, DAB, NSIA) and improving public financial management.
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Security is a major implementation consideration: (1) Civilian casualties at unprecedented levels create environment of insecurity; (2) Some areas remain difficult to access due to insecurity, affecting biometric registration and agent network development; (3) Mobile phone networks and electricity provision vulnerable to terrorism and conflict; (4) Previous experience shows elections and political instability have major negative impact on security situation; (5) Bonded trustees system for cash payments creates security risks and costs; (6) Digital payments reduce security risks by eliminating need for large cash movements on paydays. Mitigation includes: phased implementation based on security conditions, resilient system design, business continuity plans, offline processing capabilities, and multiple redundant payment channels.
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: yes - Project context includes significant displacement and refugee populations: (1) More than 1.7 million Afghans internally displaced; (2) More than 2 million Afghans have returned since 2015, mostly from Pakistan and Iran; (3) Worsening drought conditions contributing to displacement; (4) Project design includes support for digital payments as mechanism to increase financial resilience to climate change and other extreme events, which could support displaced populations and those affected by natural disasters. Digital payments platform can facilitate relief delivery by government and donors in emergency situations.
